perturbation.original_text: ensure that in the event that it enters into an electronic commerce agreement with a third party for the sale of goods on the Co-Branded Site that all agreements with the third parties shall ensure that such third party shall be wholly responsible for all products and transactions that may occur on the Co-Branded Site, that all such transactions shall comply with all applicable Canadian and US laws and regulations, including privacy laws and including compliance with MediaLinx's standards and guidelines, and ensure that such third party 2 provides a full indemnity to MediaLinx and HCI in respect of any and all transactions and products that may be hosted on the Co-Branded Site
perturbation.changed_text: ensure that in the event that it enters into an electronic commerce agreement with a third party for the sale of goods on the Co-Branded Site, that all agreements with the third parties shall endeavor to ensure that such third party is generally responsible for most products and transactions that may occur on the Co-Branded Site, and that such transactions will attempt to comply with applicable Canadian and US laws and regulations, including some privacy laws and attempting to comply with MediaLinx's standards and guidelines. Additionally, the third party will aim to provide some form of indemnity to MediaLinx and HCI in respect of a few transactions and products that may be hosted on the Co-Branded Site
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict obligation on HCI to ensure third-party e-commerce partners comply with all laws and provide full indemnity. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall ensure' with 'shall endeavor to ensure,' 'wholly responsible' with 'generally responsible for most,' 'shall comply' with 'will attempt to comply,' 'including privacy laws' with 'including some privacy laws,' 'compliance' with 'attempting to comply,' 'full indemnity' with 'some form of indemnity,' and 'any and all' with 'a few.' This introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text's vague language regarding compliance with 'some privacy laws' directly contradicts the CCPA's requirement for businesses to implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the personal information to protect the personal information. The CCPA does not allow for partial or discretionary compliance; it mandates comprehensive protection of consumer data. By suggesting that only 'some privacy laws' need to be followed, the modified text creates a scenario where a third-party vendor could fail to implement necessary safeguards, leading to a violation of the CCPA.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.100. General Duties of Businesses that Collect Personal Information (a) A business that controls the collection of a consumer’s personal information shall, at or before the point of collection, inform consumers of the following: (1) The categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of personal information are collected or used and whether that information is sold or shared. A business shall not collect additional categories of personal information or use personal information collected for additional purposes that are incompatible with the disclosed purpose for which the personal information was collected without providing the consumer with notice consistent with this section. (2) If the business collects sensitive personal information, the categories of sensitive personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of sensitive personal information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding compliance with privacy laws, which creates a contradiction with the CCPA's requirement for comprehensive data protection. The original text mandates compliance with 'all applicable Canadian and US laws and regulations, including privacy laws,' while the changed text states 'attempting to comply with applicable Canadian and US laws and regulations, including some privacy laws.' This weakening of the obligation to comply with all privacy laws contradicts the CCPA. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the CCPA gives consumers more control over their personal information and that businesses have responsibilities including 'giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices.' By suggesting only 'some privacy laws' need to be followed, the modified text allows for a scenario where a third party vendor could fail to implement necessary safeguards. Therefore, the ambiguous obligation in the changed text regarding compliance with 'some privacy laws' undermines the consumer protections intended by the CCPA as outlined in both Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2.

perturbation.original_text: HCI shall ensure that the Co-Branded Site is integrated into the Sympatico site-wide registry in order for users to have a single login access for all Sympatico.ca and Sympatico co-branded web sites.
perturbation.changed_text: HCI shall endeavor to integrate the Co-Branded Site into the Sympatico site-wide registry, aiming for users to potentially have a single login access for most Sympatico.ca and Sympatico co-branded web sites, as feasible.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a firm obligation for HCI to ensure integration for single login access. The modification introduces several ambiguities: 'shall endeavor' weakens the obligation, 'aiming for users to potentially have' makes the outcome uncertain, 'most Sympatico.ca' limits the scope, and 'as feasible' adds a subjective condition. This makes the integration goal unclear and unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: By making the single login access uncertain and limited ('potentially have a single login access for most Sympatico.ca...sites'), the modified text creates a scenario where user data might be handled inconsistently across different parts of the Sympatico network. CalOPPA requires websites to protect the confidentiality and security of consumers’ personal information. A fragmented login system where some sites require separate logins increases the risk of data breaches and inconsistent privacy practices, contradicting CalOPPA’s aim to safeguard user data through uniform and secure practices.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Codes: Search skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search Code Search Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search California Constitution - CONS Business and Professions Code - BPC Civil Code - CIV Code of Civil Procedure - CCP Commercial Code - COM Corporations Code - CORP Education Code - EDC Elections Code - ELEC Evidence Code - EVID Family Code - FAM Financial Code - FIN Fish and Game Code - FGC Food and Agricultural Code - FAC Government Code - GOV Harbors and Navigation Code - HNC Health and Safety Code - HSC Insurance Code - INS Labor Code - LAB Military and Veterans Code - MVC Penal Code - PEN Probate Code - PROB Public Contract Code - PCC Public Resources Code - PRC Public Utilities Code - PUC Revenue and Taxation Code - RTC Streets and Highways Code - SHC Unemployment Insurance Code - UIC Vehicle Code - VEH Water Code - WAT Welfare and Institutions Code - WIC
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding single login access and data handling, potentially conflicting with CalOPPA's requirements for protecting consumer data. The original text ensured integration for single login access. However, the modified text weakens this to an aim, making the outcome uncertain and limited to 'most' users. This ambiguity contradicts the Law Explanation, which states that inconsistent data handling increases the risk of data breaches, potentially failing to safeguard user data through uniform and secure practices. The scraped law snippet includes code search options, but it does not contain sufficient information to definitively support the contradiction. However, the Law explanation provides enough detail to show a contradiction exists in this case.

perturbation.original_text: All sponsorship and advertising package deals for the Co-Branded Site will be mutually agreed upon by the parties and shall abide by MediaLinx's and HCI's standard advertising agreement and Canadian and U.S. advertising regulations and standards.
perturbation.changed_text: Most sponsorship and advertising package deals for the Co-Branded Site should be generally agreed upon by the parties, and should attempt to comply with MediaLinx's and HCI's standard advertising agreement and some applicable Canadian and U.S. advertising regulations and standards.
perturbation.explanation: The original text mandates that deals 'will be mutually agreed upon' and 'shall abide by' regulations. The revised text weakens this by stating 'Most...should be generally agreed upon' and 'should attempt to comply,' introducing ambiguity and reducing the binding nature of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Trade Commission Act (FTC Act) Section 5
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that advertising deals only 'should attempt to comply' with advertising regulations, the modified text creates ambiguity about whether deceptive advertising practices are prohibited. The FTC Act Section 5 prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. By creating a loophole for advertising deals that might not fully comply, this modification is conflicting with the FTC Act, which requires advertising to be truthful and non-misleading. The ambiguity could allow for deceptive advertising that harms consumers.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Trade Commission Act | Federal Trade Commission Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Español Report Fraud Get Consumer Alerts Search the Legal Library Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original obligations to 'attempt to comply' with regulations, which introduces ambiguity about adhering to advertising standards and regulations. This directly opposes the FTC Act's mandate to prevent unfair or deceptive practices as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which empowers the FTC to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce'. The original text's 'shall abide by' is a stronger, more definitive obligation, whereas the modified text opens the door for potential non-compliance, thereby contradicting the spirit and letter of the FTC Act.
